notes.anchor: to commit it to someone whose fortune is out of reach of this war's danger
notes.note: Cicero urges that the will be entrusted to a custodian — ideally Atticus — whose property would not be confiscated should the civil war turn against the family. As a Caesarian-aligned, politically detached financier, Atticus was the natural safe pair of hands.

notes.anchor: a draft on the bank for twelve thousand sesterces
notes.note: Cicero had expected a bill of exchange (permutatio) for 12,000 sesterces, the balance of household silver-money managed by Terentia; she remitted only 10,000. The pinched sums and the suspected skimming foreshadow the couple's divorce the next year.

notes.anchor: the African business can be brought to an end
notes.note: The "African business" is the war against the Pompeian forces under Metellus Scipio, Cato, and king Juba massed in the province of Africa. Atticus hoped it might be settled by negotiation; Cicero, rightly, saw no prospect of that — Caesar crushed them at Thapsus in April 46 BC.
